annotations.label: Signed Stirling numbers of the first kind

anchor.snippet: Ordinary (signed) Stirling numbers of the first kind are commonly denoted by

annotations.note: Mathlib defines only the unsigned Stirling numbers of the first kind (Nat.stirlingFirst); the signed version (-1)^(n-k)·c(n,k) is not present.

annotations.label: Unsigned Stirling numbers of the first kind

anchor.snippet: which count the number of permutations of n elements with k disjoint cycles

annotations.note: Nat.stirlingFirst is the unsigned Stirling number of the first kind, defined by the standard recurrence and documented as counting permutations with k disjoint cycles.

annotations.label: Stirling numbers of the second kind

anchor.snippet: which count the number of ways to partition a set of n elements into k nonempty subsets

annotations.note: Nat.stirlingSecond is the Stirling number of the second kind, defined by the standard recurrence and documented as counting partitions into k nonempty subsets.

annotations.label: Falling factorial expansion

anchor.section: Expansions of falling and rising factorials
anchor.snippet: the falling factorial , defined as

annotations.note: The falling factorial is formalized as descPochhammer (and Nat.descFactorial), but its expansion as a sum of signed Stirling numbers of the first kind times powers is not formalized.

annotations.label: Empty product convention

anchor.section: Expansions of falling and rising factorials
anchor.snippet: by convention, because it is an empty product

annotations.note: descPochhammer_zero (and ascPochhammer_zero / Nat.descFactorial_zero) state the empty-product convention that the 0th falling/rising factorial equals 1.

annotations.label: Rising factorial expansion

anchor.section: Expansions of falling and rising factorials
anchor.snippet: the rising factorial , defined as

annotations.note: The rising factorial is formalized as ascPochhammer (and Nat.ascFactorial), but its expansion via unsigned Stirling numbers of the first kind is not formalized.

annotations.label: Reverse relations via second-kind numbers

anchor.section: Expansions of falling and rising factorials
anchor.snippet: Stirling numbers of the second kind express the reverse relations:

annotations.note: Both stirlingSecond and descPochhammer exist, but the identity expressing x^n as a sum of second-kind numbers times falling factorials is not formalized.

annotations.label: Polynomial sequences form bases

anchor.snippet: is a basis

annotations.note: Polynomial.basisMonomials formalizes that the monomials form a basis of R[X], but that the falling/rising factorial families are bases is not formalized.

annotations.label: Falling factorials and binomial coefficients

anchor.snippet: Falling factorials define, up to scaling, the same polynomials as binomial coefficients

annotations.note: Ring.descPochhammer_eq_factorial_smul_choose proves (x)_n = n! • C(x,n) over binomial rings (and Nat.descFactorial_eq_factorial_mul_choose over ℕ), exactly the claimed scaling relation.

annotations.label: Sum of falling factorials

anchor.snippet: the sum of falling factorials with fixed k can be expressed as another falling factorial

annotations.note: The equivalent hockey-stick identity Nat.sum_Icc_choose for binomial coefficients is formalized, but the telescoping sum of falling factorials is not stated in that form.

annotations.label: Sum of fourth powers

anchor.snippet: the sum of fourth powers of integers up to n (this time with n included), is:

annotations.note: The general Faulhaber theorem Finset.sum_range_pow (via Bernoulli numbers) is formalized, but the specific closed-form polynomial for the sum of fourth powers is not stated.

annotations.label: First and second kind are matrix inverses

anchor.snippet: The Stirling numbers of the first and second kinds can be considered inverses of one another:

annotations.note: Both kinds are defined, but their orthogonality/matrix-inverse relation (sum of products equals Kronecker delta) is not formalized.

annotations.label: Lah numbers (third kind)

anchor.snippet: are sometimes called Stirling numbers of the third kind

annotations.note: Lah numbers (Stirling numbers of the third kind) are not defined anywhere in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Lah numbers as change of basis

anchor.snippet: These numbers are coefficients expressing falling factorials in terms of rising factorials and vice versa:

annotations.note: Lah numbers and their role as change-of-basis coefficients between falling and rising factorials are not formalized.

annotations.label: Composition of changes of basis

anchor.snippet: gives the change of basis directly from

annotations.note: The composition of change-of-basis maps yielding Lah numbers is not formalized since Lah numbers are absent.

annotations.label: Lah matrices are inverse

anchor.snippet: then one is the inverse of the other

annotations.note: The mutual-inverse property of the signed/unsigned Lah matrices is not formalized.

annotations.label: Product of Stirling matrices gives Lah numbers

anchor.snippet: Composing the matrix of unsigned Stirling numbers of the first kind with the matrix of Stirling numbers of the second kind gives the Lah numbers

annotations.note: The matrix product of first- and second-kind Stirling numbers equaling Lah numbers is not formalized.

annotations.label: Enumerative definition of the three kinds

anchor.snippet: can be defined as the number of partitions of n elements into k non-empty unlabeled subsets, where each subset is endowed with no order, a cyclic order , or a linear order, respectively

annotations.note: The first and second kinds are defined (by recurrence, not enumeratively), but the unified enumerative definition and the Lah/third kind are not formalized.

annotations.note: The inequalities L(n,k) ≥ c(n,k) ≥ S(n,k) among the three kinds are not formalized (and Lah numbers are absent).

annotations.note: Both kinds are defined, but the Stirling inversion formula relating two sequences via the two kinds is not formalized.

anchor.snippet: translate into functional equations between the sequence exponential generating functions given by the Stirling (generating function) transform

annotations.note: The Stirling transform expressed as functional equations between exponential generating functions is not formalized.

annotations.note: The identities expressing (xD)^n and x^n D^n via Stirling numbers of the second/first kind are not formalized.

anchor.snippet: relate the forward differences and the ordinary

annotations.note: The forward difference operator fwdDiff is formalized, but its Stirling-number relation to ordinary derivatives is not.

anchor.snippet: Abramowitz and Stegun give the following symmetric formulae that relate the Stirling numbers of the first and second kind.

annotations.note: The Abramowitz–Stegun symmetric formulae relating the two kinds of Stirling numbers are not formalized.

anchor.snippet: Stirling numbers of first and second kind are connected by the relations:

annotations.note: Stirling numbers are defined only on ℕ in Mathlib, so the negative-index connection relations between the two kinds are not formalized.

annotations.label: Knuth's general Stirling numbers

anchor.section: Stirling numbers with negative integral values
anchor.snippet: defined the more general Stirling numbers by extending a recurrence relation to all integers

annotations.note: Knuth's extension of Stirling numbers to all integer indices is not formalized; Mathlib's definitions take ℕ arguments only.

anchor.snippet: one has the following extension of the recurrence relation of the Stirling numbers of the first kind:

annotations.note: Branson's extended recurrence for Stirling numbers of the first kind is not formalized.

anchor.snippet: one may define the negative Bell numbers by

annotations.note: Standard Bell numbers (Nat.bell) exist, but the negative Bell numbers defined via negative-index Stirling numbers are not formalized.
